title: The Anapanasati Sutta — Mindfulness of Breathing
subtitle: The 16 breath stages as a complete prāṇāyāma curriculum; the four tetrads as the four levels of the subtle body

summary.one_line: MN 118 analyzed as the foundational Buddhist prāṇāyāma system: 16 stages in four tetrads (body/feelings/mind/dharmas) mapping the practitioner's ascent through the subtle body, with the breath as t...
summary.scope: Covers the 16 breath stages, the four tetrads as four frames of reference (satipaṭṭhāna), the seven factors of awakening derived from breath practice, the clearance of knowing-and-release as the fruit.

sources.label: MN 118 — Anapanasati Sutta: Mindfulness of Breathing (Ṭhānissaro Bhikkhu trans.)

body.text: [See MN 118, tetrad 1 (body): (1) breathing long, (2) breathing short, (3) sensitive to the entire body, (4) calming bodily fabrication. 'Breathing in long, he discerns, "I am breathing in long"; or breathing out long, he discerns, "I am breathing out long."' This is the gross level: awareness of the breath as material process. The 'entire body' (sabbakāya) is the breath-body (kāya among kāyas), the prāṇamaya-kośa. The calming of bodily fabrication (passambhayaṁ kāya-saṅkhāraṁ) is the first stage of prāṇāyāma — the pacification of the breath-vāyu so that the subtle body becomes perceptible.]

[See MN 118, tetrad 2 (feelings): (5) sensitive to rapture (pīti), (6) sensitive to pleasure (sukha), (7) sensitive to mental fabrication (citta-saṅkhāra), (8) calming mental fabrication. 'He trains himself, "I will breathe in sensitive to rapture."' This is the transition from the breath-body to the feeling-body (vedanā-maya). Pīti and sukha are the prāṇa-juice — the rasa that flows through the nāḍīs when the breath is refined. Mental fabrication (saññā and vedanā per MN 44) is the manomaya-kośa. Calming mental fabrication is the entry into the second jhāna where the subtle currents of the mind-body are stilled.]

[See MN 118, tetrad 3 (mind): (9) sensitive to the mind, (10) satisfying the mind, (11) steadying the mind, (12) releasing the mind. 'He trains himself, "I will breathe in satisfying the mind."' This is the vijñānamaya-kośa — the mind itself becoming the object. The four stages of this tetrad correspond to the progressive release described in AN 9.34: the mind is step by step temporarily released from burdensome states of greater and greater refinement as it advances through the jhānas. This is the direct parallel to the Tantric purification of the citta-nāḍī.]

[See MN 118, tetrad 4 (dharmas/mental qualities): (13) focusing on inconstancy (anicca), (14) focusing on dispassion (virāga), (15) focusing on cessation (nirodha), (16) focusing on relinquishment (paṭinissagga). 'He trains himself, "I will breathe in focusing on inconstancy."' This is the ānandamaya-kośa and beyond — the seeing of the unconditioned through the conditioned. These four are the vipassanā proper, where the breath itself becomes the lens for the four Noble Truths. The sequence from anicca to paṭinissagga is a graduated dis-identification: seeing impermanence → losing attraction → ceasing grasping → relinquishing all. This is the opening to the deathless (amata-dhamma), the daimon's own ground.]

gaps: Full comparison with commentarial tradition (Vism. VIII, Paṭisambhidāmagga) needed
Cross-reference with the body-as-path doctrine from the Satipaṭṭhāna Sutta needed

issues.title: Passages need direct quotation from primary text
issues.description: Currently referencing source synthesis rather than extracting direct passages with line numbers.

issues.title: Missing visual/meditative map of the 16 stages
issues.description: A diagram showing the 16 stages mapped to the five kośas and the four jhānas would be valuable.

versions.summary: Initial RO created. 4 passages covering all four tetrads. Key themes: 16 stages as prāṇāyāma, tetrads as kośa-levels, breath as daimon vehicle.
